MCU_Arduino_Uno.question: What is the maximum safe current you can draw from a single Arduino Uno GPIO pin?
MCU_Arduino_Uno.explanation: Each GPIO pin on the ATmega328P can source/sink up to 40mA peak, but 20mA is the safe continuous limit. Exceeding this can permanently damage the pin.

MCU_Arduino_Uno.question: Which pins on the Arduino Uno are shared with I2C (SDA and SCL)?
MCU_Arduino_Uno.explanation: A4 is SDA and A5 is SCL on the Arduino Uno. If you use an I2C display (OLED or LCD), these pins are reserved and cannot be used for sensors.

MCU_Arduino_Uno.question: Why does an LED need a resistor in series when connected to Arduino?
options: To limit current and prevent burning the LED
MCU_Arduino_Uno.correct_answer: To limit current and prevent burning the LED
MCU_Arduino_Uno.explanation: An LED has very low resistance. Without a series resistor (typically 220Ω), too much current flows and the LED or GPIO pin will burn out instantly.

MCU_Arduino_Uno.question: What does the analogRead() function return on Arduino Uno?
MCU_Arduino_Uno.explanation: Arduino Uno has a 10-bit ADC, so analogRead() returns values from 0 (0V) to 1023 (5V). To convert: voltage = (value / 1023.0) * 5.0

MCU_Arduino_Uno.question: Which pins on Arduino Uno support PWM (pulse width modulation)?
MCU_Arduino_Uno.explanation: PWM pins are marked with ~ on the Arduino Uno. They are needed for servos, motor speed control, and LED dimming via analogWrite().

MCU_Arduino_Uno.question: What happens if you connect a 5V sensor output directly to an ESP32 GPIO pin?
options: The ESP32 reads the value incorrectly
It can permanently damage the ESP32 GPIO pin
MCU_Arduino_Uno.correct_answer: It can permanently damage the ESP32 GPIO pin
MCU_Arduino_Uno.explanation: ESP32 GPIO pins are only 3.3V tolerant. A 5V signal exceeds the absolute maximum rating and can destroy the pin or the entire chip. Always use a voltage divider or level shifter.

MCU_ESP32.question: What is the GPIO voltage level of the ESP32?
MCU_ESP32.explanation: The ESP32 operates at 3.3V logic. Never connect a 5V sensor directly to its GPIO — it will damage the chip. Use a voltage divider or level shifter.

MCU_ESP32.question: How does the ESP32 connect to the internet?
MCU_ESP32.explanation: The ESP32 has built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, making it ideal for IoT. No extra shield is needed — just configure your SSID and password in code.

MCU_ESP32.question: Which pins on ESP32 are INPUT ONLY and cannot be used as output?
MCU_ESP32.explanation: GPIO 34, 35, 36, and 39 are input-only pins with no internal pull-up resistors. They are perfect for analog sensors but cannot drive any output.

MCU_ESP32.question: Why do we use GPIO 34 for analog sensors in ESP32 IoT projects with WiFi active?
options: It is the fastest ADC pin
It belongs to ADC1 which works while WiFi is on
It has a built-in pull-up resistor
MCU_ESP32.correct_answer: It belongs to ADC1 which works while WiFi is on
MCU_ESP32.explanation: ESP32 has two ADCs. ADC2 pins are unusable when WiFi is active because WiFi hardware shares the same circuitry. ADC1 pins (GPIO 32-39) work reliably with WiFi on.

MCU_ESP32.question: What does 'rate limit' mean when using the Telegram Bot API?
options: The API limits how many messages you can send per second
The bot has run out of memory
MCU_ESP32.correct_answer: The API limits how many messages you can send per second
MCU_ESP32.explanation: Telegram's API allows a maximum of 30 messages per second globally. In your code, add a delay between messages to avoid being blocked.

MCU_ESP32.question: Why is the ESP32 better than Arduino Uno for IoT projects?
options: It has more RAM and built-in Wi-Fi/Bluetooth
It runs faster clock speed only
MCU_ESP32.correct_answer: It has more RAM and built-in Wi-Fi/Bluetooth
MCU_ESP32.explanation: ESP32 has 520KB RAM vs Arduino's 2KB, plus built-in Wi-Fi + Bluetooth + dual cores. This makes it perfect for running web servers, cloud communication, and complex IoT logic.

MCU_ESP32.question: What is the purpose of client.setInsecure() in ESP32 Telegram bot code?
options: It skips SSL certificate verification to fix HTTPS connection errors
MCU_ESP32.correct_answer: It skips SSL certificate verification to fix HTTPS connection errors
MCU_ESP32.explanation: Newer ESP32 Arduino cores changed how SSL certificates are handled. setInsecure() bypasses certificate validation so the HTTPS connection to Telegram's servers succeeds.

Sensor_PIR.question: What does PIR stand for in a PIR motion sensor?
Sensor_PIR.explanation: PIR stands for Passive Infrared. It detects changes in infrared heat emitted by warm bodies (like humans). 'Passive' means it doesn't emit anything — it only detects.

Sensor_PIR.question: What does the PIR sensor output when motion is detected?
Sensor_PIR.explanation: The PIR sensor sends a simple digital HIGH signal when motion is detected. You read it with digitalRead() — no complex parsing needed.

Sensor_PIR.question: Why does a PIR sensor need a few seconds to stabilise after power-on?
options: It calibrates itself to the ambient infrared level of the room
Sensor_PIR.correct_answer: It calibrates itself to the ambient infrared level of the room
Sensor_PIR.explanation: The PIR sensor compares infrared levels. At startup it needs 30-60 seconds to settle and learn the baseline room temperature. Any movement during this time causes false triggers.

Sensor_PIR.question: What are the two potentiometers on the HC-SR501 PIR module used to adjust?
Sensor_PIR.explanation: One potentiometer adjusts detection distance (3-7m). The other adjusts how long the output stays HIGH after motion is detected (few seconds to minutes).

Sensor_PIR.question: Your PIR sensor keeps triggering randomly even when there is no motion. What is the most likely cause?
options: Heat sources like sunlight, fans, or AC vents near the sensor
Sensor_PIR.correct_answer: Heat sources like sunlight, fans, or AC vents near the sensor
Sensor_PIR.explanation: PIR detects infrared heat changes. Sunlight moving through windows, air conditioning vents, or heating elements cause false triggers. Relocate the sensor away from heat sources.

Sensor_DHT11.question: What protocol does the DHT11 use to communicate with Arduino?
Sensor_DHT11.explanation: The DHT11 uses a custom single-wire protocol. You need the DHT library and connect DATA to any digital pin. It sends temperature and humidity together in one packet.

Sensor_DHT11.question: What is the temperature accuracy range of the DHT11 sensor?
Sensor_DHT11.explanation: The DHT11 has ±2°C accuracy, which is fine for basic projects. For precise measurement, use the DHT22 (±0.5°C) or a dedicated temperature IC.

Sensor_DHT11.question: What happens if you call dht.readTemperature() too frequently?
options: It returns NaN (not a number) because the sensor needs at least 1-2 seconds between reads
Sensor_DHT11.correct_answer: It returns NaN (not a number) because the sensor needs at least 1-2 seconds between reads
Sensor_DHT11.explanation: The DHT11 has a minimum sampling rate of 1 second. Reading faster causes failed reads returning NaN. Always check isnan() before using the value.

Sensor_DHT11.question: The DHT11 measures temperature range of:
Sensor_DHT11.explanation: DHT11 measures 0-50°C temperature and 20-90% humidity. For wider ranges (-40 to 80°C and 0-100% humidity), use the DHT22 sensor instead.

Sensor_DHT11.question: Why do you need a 10kΩ pull-up resistor on the DHT11 DATA pin?
options: To keep the data line HIGH when idle so the sensor can pull it LOW to send data
It is optional and makes no difference
Sensor_DHT11.correct_answer: To keep the data line HIGH when idle so the sensor can pull it LOW to send data
Sensor_DHT11.explanation: The DHT11 uses open-drain signalling — it can only pull the line LOW. A pull-up resistor keeps it HIGH when idle. Many modules have this built-in, but bare sensors need it externally.

Sensor_HC_SR04.question: How does the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor measure distance?
options: It emits a sound pulse and measures how long it takes to return
Sensor_HC_SR04.correct_answer: It emits a sound pulse and measures how long it takes to return
Sensor_HC_SR04.explanation: The HC-SR04 sends a 40kHz ultrasonic pulse via TRIG. The sound bounces off an object and returns. ECHO measures the travel time. Distance = time × speed of sound / 2.

Sensor_HC_SR04.question: Why do we divide the pulse duration by 2 when calculating distance?
options: Because the sound travels to the object AND back
Sensor_HC_SR04.correct_answer: Because the sound travels to the object AND back
Sensor_HC_SR04.explanation: The pulse time measures the round trip — to the object and back. Dividing by 2 gives the one-way distance. Formula: distance = duration × 0.034 / 2

Sensor_HC_SR04.question: The HC-SR04 ECHO pin outputs 5V. Why is this dangerous for ESP32?
options: 5V will make the sensor too hot
ESP32 GPIO pins are only 3.3V tolerant — 5V can destroy them
ESP32 cannot read digital signals above 1V
Sensor_HC_SR04.correct_answer: ESP32 GPIO pins are only 3.3V tolerant — 5V can destroy them
Sensor_HC_SR04.explanation: HC-SR04 is a 5V sensor. Its ECHO pin outputs 5V which exceeds ESP32's 3.3V GPIO limit. Use a voltage divider (1kΩ + 2kΩ) to drop ECHO to 3.3V before connecting to ESP32.

Sensor_HC_SR04.question: What is the maximum reliable detection range of the HC-SR04?
Sensor_HC_SR04.explanation: The HC-SR04 has a range of 2cm to 400cm (4 metres). Below 2cm the sensor cannot measure accurately. Beyond 4 metres the echo is too weak to detect reliably.

Sensor_LDR.question: What does LDR stand for?
Sensor_LDR.explanation: An LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) or photoresistor changes its resistance based on light intensity. Bright light → low resistance. Darkness → very high resistance (megaohms).

Sensor_LDR.question: Why is a 10kΩ resistor used alongside an LDR to read light levels on Arduino?
options: To protect the LDR from overcurrent
To form a voltage divider so the analog pin gets a readable voltage
Sensor_LDR.correct_answer: To form a voltage divider so the analog pin gets a readable voltage
Sensor_LDR.explanation: An LDR alone only changes resistance — it cannot produce a voltage. By pairing it with a fixed 10kΩ resistor in a voltage divider, the junction voltage changes with light and can be read by analogRead().

Sensor_LDR.question: In bright light, what happens to an LDR's resistance?
options: It drops to around 1kΩ or lower
Sensor_LDR.correct_answer: It drops to around 1kΩ or lower
Sensor_LDR.explanation: In bright light, an LDR's resistance drops significantly (1kΩ or below). In darkness it rises to 1MΩ or more. This resistance change is what you measure via the voltage divider.

Sensor_LDR.question: Your LDR circuit reads maximum value even in bright light. What is the most likely wiring mistake?
options: 10kΩ resistor and LDR positions swapped in the voltage divider
Sensor_LDR.correct_answer: 10kΩ resistor and LDR positions swapped in the voltage divider
Sensor_LDR.explanation: In a voltage divider, the analog pin reads the voltage at the junction. If LDR and resistor are swapped, the reading inverts — bright gives max, dark gives min. Swap their positions to fix it.

Sensor_MQ2_Gas.question: What gases can the MQ-2 sensor detect?
options: LPG, propane, methane, smoke, hydrogen, and alcohol
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.correct_answer: LPG, propane, methane, smoke, hydrogen, and alcohol
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.explanation: The MQ-2 is a broad-spectrum combustible gas sensor. It detects LPG, propane, methane, smoke, hydrogen, and alcohol vapour. It is NOT selective — it cannot distinguish between them.

Sensor_MQ2_Gas.question: Why must the MQ-2 sensor warm up for about 20 seconds before taking readings?
options: It needs to download calibration data
Its internal heating element must reach operating temperature for accurate sensing
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.correct_answer: Its internal heating element must reach operating temperature for accurate sensing
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.explanation: The MQ-2 has a resistive heating coil inside. The coil changes the sensor's resistance in the presence of gas, but only when hot. Without warmup, readings are inaccurate.

Sensor_MQ2_Gas.question: The MQ-2 heater draws approximately 150mA. Why must you NOT power it from an Arduino GPIO pin?
options: Arduino GPIO pins can only supply 20-40mA safely — 150mA will destroy the pin
Arduino cannot read analog values from it
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.correct_answer: Arduino GPIO pins can only supply 20-40mA safely — 150mA will destroy the pin
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.explanation: Always power the MQ-2 VCC pin from the 5V supply rail, not a GPIO pin. The heater current (150mA) far exceeds what a GPIO can supply and will damage the microcontroller.

Sensor_MQ2_Gas.question: What does the DO (digital output) pin on the MQ-2 module do?
options: Outputs HIGH when gas level exceeds the threshold set by the onboard potentiometer
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.correct_answer: Outputs HIGH when gas level exceeds the threshold set by the onboard potentiometer
Sensor_MQ2_Gas.explanation: The MQ-2 module has a comparator (LM393) connected to a potentiometer. When gas exceeds the threshold you set, DO goes HIGH — giving a simple digital alert without needing analogRead.

Sensor_Soil_Moisture.question: What does a lower analog reading from a soil moisture sensor indicate?
Sensor_Soil_Moisture.explanation: Wet soil conducts electricity well, so resistance is low and the analog voltage at the output is low (closer to 0). Dry soil has high resistance, giving a higher reading.

Sensor_Soil_Moisture.question: Why does a soil moisture sensor degrade over time when left in soil?
options: Electrolysis corrodes the metal probes when DC voltage is applied continuously
Sensor_Soil_Moisture.correct_answer: Electrolysis corrodes the metal probes when DC voltage is applied continuously
Sensor_Soil_Moisture.explanation: Constant DC current through the probes causes electrolytic corrosion, oxidising the metal. To extend life, only power the sensor when taking a reading using a GPIO pin for VCC.

Sensor_Soil_Moisture.question: What map() function converts a soil moisture raw reading (0-4095) to a percentage (0-100%) on ESP32?
Sensor_Soil_Moisture.explanation: Since wet soil gives LOW readings and dry gives HIGH, we invert the mapping: map(raw, 4095, 0, 0, 100). This means 4095 (dry) → 0% and 0 (wet) → 100%.

Sensor_Soil_Moisture.question: At what soil moisture percentage would you typically trigger an automatic irrigation pump?
Sensor_Soil_Moisture.explanation: Most plants need irrigation when soil moisture drops below 30%. Triggering at 10% risks plant stress. Triggering above 50% may overwater. 30% is the standard threshold for basic systems.

Sensor_Rain.question: How does a rain sensor detect rainfall?
options: By detecting conductivity between exposed copper traces when water bridges them
By measuring humidity via a capacitor
Sensor_Rain.correct_answer: By detecting conductivity between exposed copper traces when water bridges them
Sensor_Rain.explanation: The rain sensor board has a grid of exposed copper traces. When rain drops land, water bridges adjacent traces and completes a circuit, lowering resistance and changing the output voltage.

Sensor_Rain.question: What does a LOW digital output from a rain sensor module typically indicate?
Sensor_Rain.explanation: Most rain sensor modules use active-LOW logic on their DO pin. When rain is detected (resistance drops), the comparator output goes LOW. Always check your specific module's datasheet.

Sensor_Rain.question: Why should a rain sensor's control board be kept indoors while only the probe board is exposed to rain?
options: The board is too heavy for outdoors
The control board contains electronics that would be damaged by direct water exposure
Sensor_Rain.correct_answer: The control board contains electronics that would be damaged by direct water exposure
Sensor_Rain.explanation: The probe board (copper grid) is designed to get wet. The control board (with LM393 comparator and potentiometer) contains electronics that must stay dry. Only the probe is waterproof.

Sensor_Flame.question: What wavelength does a flame sensor detect?
Sensor_Flame.explanation: Flames emit strong infrared radiation in the 760-1100nm range. The flame sensor has an IR photodiode tuned to this wavelength. It can also react to other intense IR sources like sunlight.

Sensor_Flame.question: Your flame sensor keeps triggering near a window on a sunny day. Why?
options: Direct sunlight emits infrared radiation similar to flames
Sensor_Flame.correct_answer: Direct sunlight emits infrared radiation similar to flames
Sensor_Flame.explanation: Sunlight contains strong IR radiation in the same range the flame sensor detects. Always shield the sensor from direct sunlight, or use the sensor indoors or in shaded locations.

Sensor_Flame.question: What does a LOW output from a flame sensor module indicate?
Sensor_Flame.explanation: Flame sensor modules use active-LOW logic. When the IR photodiode detects flame radiation, the comparator output goes LOW. In code: if (digitalRead(FLAME_PIN) == LOW) { // flame detected }

Sensor_Flame.question: What is the typical detection angle of a flame sensor?
Sensor_Flame.explanation: Most flame sensors have a 60° detection angle. Point it directly toward the area you want to monitor. Flames outside this cone may not be detected reliably.

Sensor_Sound.question: What component inside a sound sensor module converts sound to electrical signal?
Sensor_Sound.explanation: Sound sensor modules use a small condenser (electret) microphone. Sound waves vibrate a diaphragm, changing capacitance and producing a tiny voltage signal that the LM393 comparator amplifies.

Sensor_Sound.question: What does the potentiometer on a KY-038 sound sensor module adjust?
options: The sensitivity threshold for the digital output (DO) pin
Sensor_Sound.correct_answer: The sensitivity threshold for the digital output (DO) pin
Sensor_Sound.explanation: Turning the potentiometer clockwise makes the sensor more sensitive (triggers at quieter sounds). Counter-clockwise reduces sensitivity. The AO (analog) pin always gives raw values regardless.

Sensor_Sound.question: What is the difference between the AO and DO pins on a sound sensor module?
options: AO is for Arduino, DO is for ESP32
AO gives a continuous analog voltage proportional to sound; DO gives a digital HIGH/LOW based on threshold
Sensor_Sound.correct_answer: AO gives a continuous analog voltage proportional to sound; DO gives a digital HIGH/LOW based on threshold
Sensor_Sound.explanation: AO (Analog Out) gives a raw voltage you read with analogRead() for sound level measurement. DO (Digital Out) only gives HIGH or LOW based on whether sound exceeds the potentiometer threshold.

Sensor_IR_Obstacle.question: How does an IR obstacle sensor detect objects?
options: By measuring temperature of the object
By emitting IR light and detecting its reflection from nearby objects
Sensor_IR_Obstacle.correct_answer: By emitting IR light and detecting its reflection from nearby objects
Sensor_IR_Obstacle.explanation: The IR obstacle sensor has an IR LED that emits infrared light and a photodiode that detects reflections. When an object is close enough, reflected IR hits the photodiode and triggers the output.

Sensor_IR_Obstacle.question: What does a LOW output from an IR obstacle sensor typically mean?
Sensor_IR_Obstacle.explanation: IR obstacle sensors use active-LOW logic. When an obstacle reflects IR back to the sensor, the output goes LOW. In code: if (digitalRead(IR_PIN) == LOW) { // obstacle present }

Sensor_IR_Obstacle.question: Why might an IR obstacle sensor fail to detect black or dark objects?
options: Black surfaces absorb IR light instead of reflecting it back
The sensor only works at night
Dark objects are outside detection frequency
Sensor_IR_Obstacle.correct_answer: Black surfaces absorb IR light instead of reflecting it back
Sensor_IR_Obstacle.explanation: Black and dark surfaces absorb infrared light rather than reflecting it. The sensor relies on reflection — if there is no reflection, it cannot detect the object even if it is very close.

Sensor_IR_Obstacle.question: What does the potentiometer on an IR obstacle sensor module adjust?
Sensor_IR_Obstacle.explanation: Turning the potentiometer adjusts how far the sensor can detect objects. The detection range is typically adjustable from about 2cm to 30cm depending on the module.

Sensor_Heartbeat.question: How does a pulse/heartbeat sensor measure heart rate?
options: By shining LED light through the finger and detecting blood volume changes (photoplethysmography)
By detecting electrical signals from the heart
Sensor_Heartbeat.correct_answer: By shining LED light through the finger and detecting blood volume changes (photoplethysmography)
Sensor_Heartbeat.explanation: Pulse sensors use photoplethysmography (PPG). A green or IR LED shines through the fingertip. Each heartbeat increases blood volume, absorbing more light. A photodiode detects these rhythmic changes.

Sensor_Heartbeat.question: Why does a pulse sensor give noisy or unstable readings?
options: Movement of the finger, inconsistent pressure, or ambient light interference
Sensor_Heartbeat.correct_answer: Movement of the finger, inconsistent pressure, or ambient light interference
Sensor_Heartbeat.explanation: Pulse sensors are very sensitive to motion artifacts. Keep the finger still, apply consistent moderate pressure, and shield from bright ambient light. Averaging multiple samples also reduces noise.

Sensor_Heartbeat.question: What is the typical output of a heartbeat sensor connected to an analog pin?
options: A waveform that peaks with each heartbeat
Sensor_Heartbeat.correct_answer: A waveform that peaks with each heartbeat
Sensor_Heartbeat.explanation: The sensor outputs a continuous analog voltage that rises and falls with each heartbeat. You read this with analogRead() and look for peaks in the waveform to count beats per minute.

Sensor_Temperature_LM35.question: How does the LM35 convert temperature to a readable output?
options: It outputs an analog voltage of 10mV per degree Celsius
Sensor_Temperature_LM35.correct_answer: It outputs an analog voltage of 10mV per degree Celsius
Sensor_Temperature_LM35.explanation: The LM35 outputs exactly 10mV per °C. At 25°C it outputs 250mV. At 0°C it outputs 0V. This linear relationship makes conversion simple: tempC = voltage × 100.

Sensor_Temperature_LM35.question: At 30°C, what voltage does the LM35 output?
Sensor_Temperature_LM35.explanation: LM35 outputs 10mV per °C. At 30°C: 30 × 10mV = 300mV (0.3V). On ESP32 (3.3V reference, 12-bit ADC): rawValue = 0.3 / 3.3 × 4095 ≈ 372. Then tempC = rawValue × (3.3/4095) × 100.

Sensor_Temperature_LM35.question: What is the main advantage of LM35 over DHT11 for temperature measurement?
options: LM35 gives continuous analog output without delays between reads
Sensor_Temperature_LM35.correct_answer: LM35 gives continuous analog output without delays between reads
Sensor_Temperature_LM35.explanation: Unlike DHT11 which needs 1-2 seconds between reads, LM35 gives a continuous real-time analog voltage. You can sample it as fast as needed — ideal for data logging and control systems.

Sensor_Temperature_LM35.question: Why must you use a 3.3V reference when reading LM35 on an ESP32 instead of 5V?
options: ESP32 ADC reference is 3.3V — using 5V power source changes the voltage formula
Sensor_Temperature_LM35.correct_answer: ESP32 ADC reference is 3.3V — using 5V power source changes the voltage formula
Sensor_Temperature_LM35.explanation: If LM35 is powered by 5V but ESP32 ADC reference is 3.3V, readings above 33°C would exceed 3.3V input limit and saturate the ADC. Power LM35 from ESP32's 3.3V pin for correct readings.

Actuator_LED.question: If your LED glows dimly, what is the most likely cause?
Actuator_LED.explanation: A high resistance limits current too much, making the LED dim. Try reducing the resistor from 1kΩ to 220Ω. The formula is R = (Vsupply - Vled) / Iled.

Actuator_LED.question: What is the forward voltage drop of a typical red LED?
Actuator_LED.explanation: Red LEDs drop approximately 2V across them. Blue and white LEDs drop about 3-3.2V. This voltage must be subtracted when calculating the correct series resistor value.

Actuator_LED.question: You connect an LED directly to GPIO without a resistor. What happens?
options: LED and/or GPIO pin burns out due to excessive current
Actuator_LED.correct_answer: LED and/or GPIO pin burns out due to excessive current
Actuator_LED.explanation: Without a resistor, the only resistance is the GPIO's internal resistance (~25Ω on ESP32). Ohm's law: I = (3.3V - 2V) / 25Ω = 52mA — far exceeding the 12mA GPIO limit of ESP32.

Actuator_LED.question: Which leg of an LED is the anode (positive) and how do you identify it?
options: The shorter leg is the anode
The longer leg is the anode
The flat side is the anode
Actuator_LED.correct_answer: The longer leg is the anode
Actuator_LED.explanation: The longer leg is the anode (+) and connects toward VCC/GPIO. The shorter leg is the cathode (-) and connects to GND. The flat edge on the LED body also marks the cathode side.

Actuator_Relay_5V.question: What is the primary purpose of a relay in an electronics project?
options: To allow a low-power microcontroller to switch high-power devices on and off
Actuator_Relay_5V.correct_answer: To allow a low-power microcontroller to switch high-power devices on and off
Actuator_Relay_5V.explanation: A relay is an electrically operated switch. A small 5V signal from Arduino/ESP32 energises a coil which mechanically closes contacts that can handle 250VAC/10A — completely isolated from the MCU.

Actuator_Relay_5V.question: Most 5V relay modules are ACTIVE LOW. What does this mean in code?
options: digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, HIGH) turns the relay ON
digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW) turns the relay ON
You need PWM to control it
Actuator_Relay_5V.correct_answer: digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW) turns the relay ON
Actuator_Relay_5V.explanation: Active-LOW relay modules trigger (close the switch) when the input is pulled LOW. This is because the driver transistor on the module conducts when its base is pulled low. Always check your module datasheet.

Actuator_Relay_5V.question: Why should you NEVER connect mains voltage (220V AC) wiring without proper insulation when using a relay module?
options: It will burn the relay coil
Mains voltage is lethal — improper connections cause fatal electric shock or fire
The relay module cannot handle AC
Actuator_Relay_5V.correct_answer: Mains voltage is lethal — improper connections cause fatal electric shock or fire
Actuator_Relay_5V.explanation: The relay's contact side can switch mains voltage, but mains electricity at 220V/50Hz is potentially lethal. Always ensure mains wiring is properly insulated, use a fused circuit, and never touch live wires.

Actuator_Relay_5V.question: Why does a relay module need its own 5V power supply and NOT run from an ESP32's 3.3V GPIO pin?
options: The relay coil draws 70mA — far more than an ESP32 GPIO can supply (12mA max)
ESP32 and relay use different grounds
Actuator_Relay_5V.correct_answer: The relay coil draws 70mA — far more than an ESP32 GPIO can supply (12mA max)
Actuator_Relay_5V.explanation: The relay's electromagnetic coil draws 70mA when energised. ESP32 GPIO pins can only supply 12mA. The module's built-in transistor driver handles this — connect VCC to 5V supply, not GPIO.

Actuator_Buzzer.question: What is the difference between an active and passive buzzer?
options: Active buzzers beep with just DC power; passive buzzers need PWM tone
Actuator_Buzzer.correct_answer: Active buzzers beep with just DC power; passive buzzers need PWM tone
Actuator_Buzzer.explanation: An active buzzer has an internal oscillator — connect power and it beeps. A passive buzzer needs a tone() function with a frequency. Active is easier; passive is more flexible.

Actuator_Buzzer.question: How do you make a passive buzzer play a specific musical note on Arduino?
Actuator_Buzzer.explanation: The tone() function generates a PWM square wave at the specified frequency (in Hz). Middle C is 262Hz. To stop: noTone(BUZZER_PIN). This only works for passive buzzers — active buzzers ignore frequency.

Actuator_Buzzer.question: Why does a buzzer connected directly to GPIO sometimes sound weak on ESP32?
options: ESP32 GPIO output voltage (3.3V) is lower than 5V buzzers need for full volume
Actuator_Buzzer.correct_answer: ESP32 GPIO output voltage (3.3V) is lower than 5V buzzers need for full volume
Actuator_Buzzer.explanation: Most 5V buzzers produce less volume when driven at 3.3V. For louder output, use a transistor (2N2222 or BC547) to drive the buzzer from a 5V supply, with the GPIO controlling the transistor base.

Actuator_Servo_SG90.question: What is the angle range of the SG90 servo motor?
Actuator_Servo_SG90.explanation: The SG90 is a positional servo with 0-180° range. Use myServo.write(angle) to set position. For continuous rotation, you need a different servo type.

Actuator_Servo_SG90.question: Why should a servo motor be powered separately from Arduino 5V pin?
options: Servos draw too much current and can reset or damage the Arduino
Actuator_Servo_SG90.correct_answer: Servos draw too much current and can reset or damage the Arduino
Actuator_Servo_SG90.explanation: An SG90 servo can draw 200-700mA under load. The Arduino 5V pin can only supply ~500mA total. Under heavy load, this can reset the Arduino or damage the voltage regulator.

Actuator_Servo_SG90.question: What type of signal does a servo motor use to determine its position?
options: PWM signal with 50Hz frequency and 1-2ms pulse width
Actuator_Servo_SG90.correct_answer: PWM signal with 50Hz frequency and 1-2ms pulse width
Actuator_Servo_SG90.explanation: Servos use PWM at 50Hz (20ms period). A 1ms pulse = 0°, 1.5ms = 90°, 2ms = 180°. The Servo library handles this automatically — you just call myServo.write(angle).

Actuator_Servo_SG90.question: What happens if you call myServo.write(200) on an SG90?
options: Servo moves to maximum 180° and may stall, drawing excess current
Actuator_Servo_SG90.correct_answer: Servo moves to maximum 180° and may stall, drawing excess current
Actuator_Servo_SG90.explanation: Values above 180° force the servo to its mechanical limit. It strains against the stop, drawing high stall current continuously. Always clamp values: angle = constrain(angle, 0, 180).

Actuator_Water_Pump.question: Why must a water pump be controlled through a relay or transistor, not directly from a GPIO pin?
options: Pumps draw 200-300mA which destroys GPIO pins limited to 20-40mA
Actuator_Water_Pump.correct_answer: Pumps draw 200-300mA which destroys GPIO pins limited to 20-40mA
Actuator_Water_Pump.explanation: A mini 5V water pump draws 200-300mA. GPIO pins can only source 20-40mA. Connecting directly would instantly destroy the GPIO or the entire MCU. Always use a relay or transistor switch.

Actuator_Water_Pump.question: In an automatic plant watering system, what sensor is typically paired with a water pump?
Actuator_Water_Pump.explanation: The soil moisture sensor measures water content in soil. When it drops below a threshold (e.g. 30%), the MCU activates the water pump via relay. This is the standard automatic irrigation pattern.

Actuator_Water_Pump.question: What is the risk of running a submersible water pump dry (without water)?
options: The impeller and motor overheat and burn out permanently
Actuator_Water_Pump.correct_answer: The impeller and motor overheat and burn out permanently
Actuator_Water_Pump.explanation: Submersible pumps are cooled and lubricated by the water they pump. Running dry causes rapid overheating of the motor windings and impeller bearings, permanently destroying the pump.

Display_OLED_SSD1306.question: What is the I2C address commonly used by the SSD1306 OLED display?
Display_OLED_SSD1306.explanation: Most 0.96" SSD1306 OLED displays use I2C address 0x3C. Some use 0x3D. You can scan with a simple I2C scanner sketch if your display doesn't work.

Display_OLED_SSD1306.question: Which pins on ESP32 are used for I2C communication with the OLED?
Display_OLED_SSD1306.explanation: By default, ESP32 uses GPIO 21 for SDA and GPIO 22 for SCL. These are the hardware I2C pins. Multiple I2C devices (OLED, LCD, sensors) share the same SDA/SCL bus.

Display_OLED_SSD1306.question: How much current does the SSD1306 OLED display draw at maximum brightness?
Display_OLED_SSD1306.explanation: The SSD1306 OLED draws up to 30mA at full brightness with all pixels on. This is well within GPIO power budget and can be powered from the 3.3V supply pin directly.

Display_LCD_16x2.question: What does the 'I2C' module on the back of an LCD 16x2 do?
options: Reduces 16 control pins to just 2 (SDA + SCL)
Display_LCD_16x2.correct_answer: Reduces 16 control pins to just 2 (SDA + SCL)
Display_LCD_16x2.explanation: The I2C backpack (PCF8574) acts as a GPIO expander. Instead of connecting 6-16 wires to the LCD, you only need SDA (A4) and SCL (A5) — saving precious pins.

Display_LCD_16x2.question: Your LCD shows garbled characters or random symbols. What is the most likely cause?
options: Wrong I2C address in the library
Display_LCD_16x2.correct_answer: Wrong I2C address in the library
Display_LCD_16x2.explanation: LCD I2C backpacks use either address 0x27 or 0x3F depending on the PCF8574 chip variant. If the address in your code doesn't match, the LCD shows garbage. Run an I2C scanner to find the correct address.

Display_LCD_16x2.question: How do you clear the LCD screen in code?
Display_LCD_16x2.explanation: lcd.clear() clears all characters and moves the cursor to position (0,0). Note: it takes about 2ms to execute. For faster updates, overwrite characters with spaces instead of calling clear() repeatedly.

Input_Button.question: What is a floating pin and why is it a problem with buttons?
options: A pin that is not connected to anything and gives random HIGH/LOW readings
A pin that outputs AC voltage
A pin connected to both VCC and GND
A pin that only works at high frequency
Input_Button.correct_answer: A pin that is not connected to anything and gives random HIGH/LOW readings
Input_Button.explanation: When a button is open (not pressed), an unconnected GPIO pin picks up electrical noise and reads randomly. Pull-up or pull-down resistors hold the pin at a defined voltage until the button changes it.

Input_Button.question: When using INPUT_PULLUP mode on Arduino/ESP32, what value does digitalRead() return when the button is NOT pressed?
Input_Button.explanation: INPUT_PULLUP connects an internal resistor from the pin to VCC, holding it HIGH. When the button connects the pin to GND, it reads LOW. So button pressed = LOW, button released = HIGH.

Input_Button.question: What is 'button debouncing' and why is it necessary?
options: Filtering out rapid false triggers caused by mechanical bouncing when a button is pressed
Adding a delay before the button responds
Input_Button.correct_answer: Filtering out rapid false triggers caused by mechanical bouncing when a button is pressed
Input_Button.explanation: When a button is pressed, metal contacts bounce 5-20 times in milliseconds before settling. Without debouncing, a single press registers as many presses. Fix with a 10-50ms delay or a debounce library.

Input_Potentiometer.question: What does a potentiometer do in a circuit?
options: Acts as a variable resistor to provide an adjustable voltage output
Input_Potentiometer.correct_answer: Acts as a variable resistor to provide an adjustable voltage output
Input_Potentiometer.explanation: A potentiometer has a resistive track and a sliding wiper. As you turn the knob, the wiper moves along the track, changing the ratio of resistance and producing a variable voltage from 0V to VCC.

Input_Potentiometer.question: A potentiometer has 3 pins. How should they be connected to read its value on ESP32?
options: Outer pins to 3.3V and GND, middle (wiper) pin to analog GPIO
Middle pin to 5V, outer pins to GND
Input_Potentiometer.correct_answer: Outer pins to 3.3V and GND, middle (wiper) pin to analog GPIO
Input_Potentiometer.explanation: The two outer pins connect to power (3.3V on ESP32) and ground. The middle wiper pin connects to an analog input pin. Turning the knob moves the wiper, changing the voltage the ADC reads.

Input_Potentiometer.question: analogRead() on ESP32 returns 0-4095 for a potentiometer. What does 2048 represent approximately?
Input_Potentiometer.explanation: ESP32's 12-bit ADC gives 0-4095. 0 = GND (fully counterclockwise), 4095 = 3.3V (fully clockwise), 2048 ≈ 1.65V = middle position. Use map(val, 0, 4095, 0, 100) to convert to percentage.
